the right to license and house their products that they can kill at any given time.

paragraphs.text: Hello I'm sure you are probably already aware of the Stop Killing Games movement, and I would like you to consider this proposal. The business practice of developers being able to just disable a product they have already sold to consumers, without any precautions or refunds, is just outrageous. The idea of selling a product and then disabling the same product at the will of the producer without any thought to the buyer who has paid to buy (and should therefore own said product) should be considered thievery. Making money without service. Please remember that this is not just about games and angry gamers, it's about business practice. If this practice is shown to be perfectly okay, how long will it take before other fields of businesses consider using the same methods? Imagine the producer of your television suddenly decides to discontinue supporting that exact TV model, and then suddenly you just can't turn it on anymore. "Well, that's a shame, but we have decided this model is no longer worth supporting. How about you buying this new one instead? It's only twice as expensive, and you get half the features!" This is the practice right now for games, because there is no control over these companies and no one cares to consider the consumers. I know this is currently about games, but as I said, it's really about a business model. If this is shown to be perfectly alright, other businesses will follow over time. Just one toe at a time, until they walk the same path of tricking you, the consumer, out of your money by paying them fortunes for the right to license and house their products that they can kill at any given time. They want you to go out and buy a new one instead. Lasting for how long until they decide to shut it down again. So please consider this for the Digital Fainess Act.
